title: Node HTTP Primitives, Connections, Protocols, Timeouts, and Shutdown · Narrowing Workshop
summary: A backend pairing room predicts runtime, request, middleware, dependency, response, and cleanup transitions before assembling the first Node HTTP server protocol and timeout budget for node http primitives, connections, protocols, timeouts, and shutdown. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of Node HTTP server protocol and timeout budget while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and…
objectives: Trace IncomingMessage and ServerResponse as streams with header commitment, body ownership, backpressure, abort, error, and close lifetimes.
Configure requestTimeout, headersTimeout, keepAliveTimeout, keepAliveTimeoutBuffer, maxHeadersCount, and maximum header size from a threat and latency budget.
Distinguish HTTP/1.1, Node HTTP/2 compatibility behavior, ALPN, TLS, upgrades, CONNECT, and the explicit external boundary for HTTP/3.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Node and Express server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Node Request Response Streams
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop team handles case 4022 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to gate protocol support with explicit header, request, and drain policy; keep peak memory below the stated byte ceiling; then fail the canary while the old revision drains and prove rollback remains safe. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Node and Express server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Node Request Response Streams.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for trace incomingmessage and serverresponse as streams with header commitment, body ownership, backpressure, abort, error, and close lifetimes. For Node and Express server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Node Request Response Streams, keep Node Request Response Streams connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

hints: For Node and Express server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Protocol Transfer Evidence, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Protocol Transfer Evidence precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Node and Express server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Protocol Transfer Evidence, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop team handles case 1166 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to gate protocol support with explicit header, request, and drain policy; keep peak memory below the stated byte ceiling; then abort during a dependency phase and preserve cleanup plus response ownership. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Require live TLS identity, proxy, slow-client, protocol negotiation, upgrade, drain, and packet-level evidence beyond deterministic browser models.

For Node and Express server Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Protocol Transfer Evidence, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A pure request-decision function proves listener and wire-protocol behavior.

steps.title: Node and Express server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Protocol Transfer Evidence
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Protocol Transfer Evidence: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop team handles case 6023 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to gate protocol support with explicit header, request, and drain policy; keep peak memory below the stated byte ceiling; then saturate one tenant budget while another tenant remains serviceable. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Require live TLS identity, proxy, slow-client, protocol negotiation, upgrade, drain, and packet-level evidence beyond deterministic browser models.

Evidence target: Node and Express server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Protocol Transfer Evidence.
steps.why: Retrieving Protocol Transfer Evidence through the concrete Node HTTP server protocol and timeout budget case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide.
